coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescent tag based on dual-emissive hydrophobic carbon dots for in-situ monitoring of seafood freshness

Ma, Ji

Tang, Ben Zhong
coredata.dc:description: A ratiometric fluorescent tag based on dual-emissive hydrophobic carbon dots (H-CDs) with well volatile base nitrogens (VBNs)-response was fabricated, offering the in-situ, real-time, and visual evaluation of seafood freshness. The presented H-CDs aggregates exhibited a sensitive response to VBNs, with a limit of detection of 7 μM and 137 ppb for spermine and ammonia hydroxide, respectively. Subsequently, a ratiometric tag was successfully fabricated by depositing dual-emissive CDs on cotton paper. Upon the treatment with ammonia vapour, the presented tag exhibited highly recognizable colour variations from red to blue under UV light. In addition, the cytotoxicity was explored by CCK8 assay, and the results proved the nontoxicity of the presented H-CDs. To our knowledge, this is the first ratiometric tag based on dual-emissive CDs with aggregation-induced emission properties for recognition of VBNs and seafood freshness in a real-time and visual manner.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A ratiometric fluorescent tag based on dual-emissive hydrophobic carbon dots (H-CDs) with well volatile base nitrogens (VBNs)-response was fabricated, offering the in-situ, real-time, and visual evaluation of seafood freshness. The presented H-CDs aggregates exhibited a sensitive response to VBNs, with a limit of detection of 7 μM and 137 ppb for spermine and ammonia hydroxide, respectively. Subsequently, a ratiometric tag was successfully fabricated by depositing dual-emissive CDs on cotton paper. Upon the treatment with ammonia vapour, the presented tag exhibited highly recognizable colour variations from red to blue under UV light. In addition, the cytotoxicity was explored by CCK8 assay, and the results proved the nontoxicity of the presented H-CDs. To our knowledge, this is the first ratiometric tag based on dual-emissive CDs with aggregation-induced emission properties for recognition of VBNs and seafood freshness in a real-time and visual manner.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Rich nutrition and high moisture make seafood highly spoilage, mainly caused by microbial growth and biochemical reaction during the process of storage, transportation and sale. Every year, about 46 million tons or a third to a half of seafood are wasted in the food supply chain, causing serious financial loss [1–5]. Thus, to minimize food spoilage, it is necessary to develop a rapid and effective method for monitoring quality and freshness in the seafood supply chain. Up to now, routine analytical methods employed to monitor food freshness are based on the evaluation of sensory, microbial and physiochemical properties [1,6–9]. However, the shortcoming of these analytical methods is tedious operation, long testing period, and destruction of sample due to pretreatment, making it hard to realize rapid and non-invasive monitoring of seafood freshness [10–12].

Packaging is an indispensable part of preparing seafood for transport, storage, sale, and end-use, which can prevent contamination and mechanical injury, maintain seafood quality, and prolong its shelf life [7,13–15]. Particularly, intelligent packages, as a promising class of food packaging technology, can detect, sense, record, and trace the internal or external variations of the products, and then provide information about the seafood quality and freshness during the food supply chain [7,16–18]. Food freshness indicators (FFI) are a kind of intelligent package, which can directly monitor the freshness of packaging products by reacting with metabolites produced in seafood due to microbial growth or metabolism [16,19–22]. Among various sensors employed in the FFI system, fluorescent indicators, mainly including indicator cards [23,24] and indicator film [24], have attracted increasing attention with the help of good sensitivity, ease-of-use, as well as flexibility in real-time and visual monitoring [25–29].

Featuring excellent photophysical properties and low toxicity, carbon dots (CDs), as a promising form of luminescent nanomaterials, have elicited ripples of excitement in the evaluation of seafood quality and freshness because they can be designed or post-renovated to react with target [30–35]. In this context, several pH-sensitive CDs with VBNs-responsive properties, such as N-doped CDs [30], red-emitting CDs [32], and carboxy-functional CDs [36], had been designed and applied for monitoring food freshness. But these reported CDs-based sensing systems that depend on single-wavelength fluorescence perform poorly due to the negative effects of background interference. Thus, for improving the useability of these fluorescent tags with only one fluorophor, studies on ratiometric tags are attracted more interest. To fabricate ratiometric fluorescent probes, an effective strategy is to combine the single-emissive CDs with other fluorescent materials. Based on it, CDs/CdTe quantum dots (CdTe QDs) [37], lignin-doped r-CDs/lignin-doped b-CDs [38], and dye-incorporated CDs [39] had been synthesized as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for monitoring VBNs. For instance, Qin et al. synthesized dye-incorporated CDs to fabricate a ratiometric fluorescent strip for in-situ evaluation of fish freshness [39]. However, the preparation process for these probes is tedious and time-consuming to some extent. Recent studies have confirmed that the design of CDs with dual-emission can effectively decline and eliminate background interference [33,40–42]. For example, Cui et al. prepared CDs with dual-emission properties and used them to detect coenzyme A, and results revealed that this ratiometric probe exhibited a good sensitivity for monitoring coenzyme A in living cells [40]. Liu et al. successfully synthesized red/green dual-emissive CDs in one step by using 2,3-diaminotouluene sulfate as a carbon source [42]. However, most reported CDs show weak emission in the solid and aggregated state, which limits their actual applications in the field of FFI. Fortunately, the emergence of aggregation-induced emission (AIE) offers a new avenue to construct a ratiometric fluorescent probe based on dual-emissive CDs [43–46]. Xu et al. synthesized CDs with multicolour AIE emission using dithiosalicylic acid (DTSA) and amine molecules via a one-pot solvothermal method [44]. Jiang et al. reported a one-pot solvothermal method for the construction of dual-emissive CDs with AIE properties through trimellitic acid as a carbon source [43]. However, despite the above research, no studies are available on using dual-emissive CDs as indicators to fabricate a ratiometric probe for monitoring seafood freshness.

Therefore, this study aimed to fabricate a ratiometric tag for in-situ recognition of seafood freshness by naked eyes, and the specific work was arranged as follows: (1) dual-emissive hydrophobic CDs (H-CDs) aggregates was prepared and employed to detect VBNs with ammonium hydroxide and spermine as typical targets; (2) the ratiometric tag was fabricated and used to monitor ammonia vapour for assessing the seafood freshness; (3) the cytotoxicity was explored by CCK8 assay to confirm the safety of the as-prepared H-CDs. The process of preparation and monitoring of VBNs of this ratiometric tag was revealed in Fig. 1 . This work might offer the avenue to the rational design of dual-emissive CDs-based ratiometric fluorescent tags.

Melamine (MA, 99%), dithiosalicylic acid (DTSA, 96%), spermine (98%), triethylamine (TEA, 99.5%), methanol (MeOH, 99.5%), acetic acid (99.8%), ethanol (EtOH, 99.8%), hydrogen sulfide (H2S, 100.0 μg/mL), methanal (HCHO, 37.0%), isopropanol (IPA, 99.9%) and ethyl acetate (EtOAc, 99.5%) were purchased from Aladdin Bio-Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ammonium hydroxide solution (NH3, 30.0–33.0% NH3 in H2O), histamine (97.0%), tyramine (98.0%), cadaverine (96.5%), spermidine (99.0%), and putrescine (98.5%) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd. (St. Louis, MO, USA). Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were purchased from Invitrogen Corp. (Carlsbad, CA). Cell proliferation reagent CCK8 was obtained from Life Corp. (Basel, Switzerland). All reagents were of analytical grade. All solutions were prepared with deionized water (18.2 MΩ) from a Milli-Q system (Millipore). Fresh greasy-back shrimp (Metapenaeus ensis) were purchased from a local supermarket in Guangzhou, China.

Fluorescence spectra were taken by an RF-6000 Spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Tokyo, Japan), and all the fluorescent images were taken using a smartphone at 365 nm UV light (Xiaomi Co., China). Ultraviolet (UV)-vis absorption data were obtained using a UV-1800 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Tokyo, Japan). The morphologies of H-CDs were acquired by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) Talos an F200X system (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., USA). The binding energy spectra were measured on a VG ESCA210 X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Kratos Analytical Ltd., UK). The surface morphology of the fibre cotton paper and the loaded H-CDs aggregates tag were acquired using a scan electron microscope (SEM, Carl Zeiss NTS GmbH, Germany). The infrared (IR) data was obtained by a Nicolet Continuum infrared spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Waltham, USA). The absolute quantum yields (QY) of the H-CDs and H-CDs aggregates were observed using an absolute photoluminescence quantum yield spectrometer C11347 Quantaurus_QY (Hamamatsu Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was tested on a Zetasizer Nano Series (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK). X-ray diffraction (XRD) dates of the H-CDs aggregates with and without VBNs were obtained using an X-ray diffractometer (PANalytical B.V., Almelo Netherlands). Absorbance was tested in a cell cytotoxicity assay using a microplate reader (Bio-Rad, Model 550, USA). The Raman spectra were taken by a LabRAM HR (Horiba France SAS, Villeneuve d’Ascq, France). The atomic ratios (C, H, N, O) of the H-CDs aggregates with and without VBNs were obtained using elemental analyzer (Elementar Analysensysteme GmbH, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of H-CDs and H-CDs aggregates
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis method of H-CDs was minor adapted from a reported method [45]. Typically, 0.403 g of MA and 1.088 g of DTSA were completely dispersed in 80 mL of acetic acid by sonication treatment. Subsequently, the obtained solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and kept at 180 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained solution was added to 1.5 L of boiling deionized water to wash the excess raw material and solvent. The purified H-CDs were acquired through vacuum filtration and vacuum-dried at 50 °C for 2 d.

To prepare H-CDs aggregates, 40 mg of H-CDs was dispersed in 10 mL ethanol (EtOH) solution to obtain H-CDs solution (4 mg/mL), and then water was added to 1 mL of H-CDs solution to acquire a final volume of 10 mL. After shaking the mixture, the H-CDs aggregates at 0.4 mg/mL were achieved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of loaded H-CDs aggregates tags
ce:section.ce:para: The schematic diagram of the preparation of loaded H-CDs aggregates tag was depicted in Fig. 1. Specifically, the fibre cotton paper was cut into a circle with a diameter of 1 cm, and then the H-CDs solution (4 mg/mL, 100 μL) was dropped on the surface of the fibre cotton paper. Afterwards, the soaked circle fibre cotton papers were dried at 50 °C for 4 h to prepare the loaded H-CDs aggregates tag and stored in a refrigerator (4 °C) for the following experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of VBNs with H-CDs aggregates
ce:section.ce:para: Different concentrations of 200 μL of biogenic amines (50, 100, 200, 400, 500, and 1000 μM) were mixed with 800 μL of H-CDs aggregates (0.4 mg/mL) solution for 8 min, and then transferred for fluorescence measurements.

Different concentrations of 200 μL of ammonium hydroxide (2, 20, 60, 80, 100, and 300 ppm) were mixed with 800 μL of H-CDs aggregates (0.4 mg/mL) solution for 8 min and then transferred for fluorescence measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring VBNs vapour using the loaded H-CDs aggregates tags
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the fabricated loaded H-CDs aggregates tag was studied before investigating the sensitivity of the as-prepared tag toward VBNs. Three millilitres of stock solution of NH3 (4000 ppm), EtOH, H2S, H2O, MeOH, TEA, EtOAc, and IPA were placed with the as-prepared tag, respectively. After storage for 3 min, the fluorescence colour changes of these tags were observed by a smartphone under UV light.

To detect ammonia vapour, different concentrations of ammonium hydroxide and the as-prepared tag were placed on a 35 mm plastic culture dish, respectively. The fluorescence colour changes of these tags were taken within 2 min with an interval of 30 s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring of VBNs in shrimp using the loaded H-CDs aggregates tag
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the fluorescence response of the loaded H-CDs aggregates tags to VBNs, as depicted in Fig. 1, the as-prepared tag was placed with 50 ± 0.75 g of shrimps in the sealed petri dish (150 mm diameter) and then stored at two temperatures (4, and 28 °C) to produce VBNs. The variations of the fluorescence colour of the fabricated tags were taken by a smartphone. For comparison, the total VBNs values in shrimps were measured according to the E.U. Commission Regulation, which stipulated that the acceptable limit of the total VBNs value for shrimp is 30 mg/100 g.

The cytotoxicity of the fabricated H-CDs against 293T cells was investigated by using the CCK8 assay. 293T cells were seeded at 2.0 × 104 cells/well in 96-well plates and cultured in DMEM with 10% FBS for 24 h. Then, H-CDs with known concentrations dispersed in DMEM with 10% FBS were added to each well. After being incubated for 24 h, 10 μL of CCK8 reagent was added into each well and then incubated for another 2 h. Finally, the absorbance was tested at 450 nm using a microplate reader. Generally, the cell proliferation level could be measured by the optical density (OD) value, and the relative cell viabilities (%) were measured as follows:
(1)
C e l l
v i a b i l i t e s
( % )

where ODsample and ODcontrol were measured in the presence and absence of H-CDs, respectively.
All the measurements were performed in triplicate and the obtained results were analyzed using OriginPro 8.5 software (OriginLab Co., Northampton, USA).

The structure properties, elemental composition, and morphology of the as-prepared H-CDs were explored by using IR, XPS, XRD, Raman, and TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 A, there are hydrophobic groups containing C – S (695 cm−1), C – O (1147 cm−1), aromatic C – NH (1265 cm−1), C – N (1415 cm−1), C = C (1464 cm−1), amide carbonyl (1682 cm−1), S – H (2657 cm−1), and methylene (2966 cm−1) on the surface of the H-CDs. While the hydrophilic functional group such as the amino or hydroxyl group (3418 cm−1 and 3067 cm−1) being from the MA and DTSA completely disappeared on the surface of H-CDs, indicating the hydrophobic property of the H-CDs [45,47].

The XPS was used to explore the elemental species of the H-CDs. Fig. 2B shows the full XPS survey spectrum of the H-CDs, revealing the presence of O (1s), N (1s), C (1s), S (2s and 2p). The corresponding atomic ratios were 34.34%, 0.95%, 56.82% and 7.89%, respectively [45,47]. Besides, the spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 2C) can be deconvolved into three peaks at 284.68, 285.51, and 289.18 eV, attributing to C – C/C = C, C – N, and C = O/C = N, respectively [48]. The spectrum of S 2p (Fig. 2D) exhibited three peaks at 163.4, 163.8, and 164.4 eV, respectively, attributing to S – C, S – H, and S – S [47]. Meanwhile, the spectrum of N 1s displayed two peaks at 399.07 eV for pyridinic C3 – N and 400.27 eV for pyrrolic C2 – N – H groups (Fig. 2E) [48]. As revealed in Fig. 2F, the XRD pattern of the H-CDs showed that the diffraction peaks were 26° and 41°, representing the interlayer spacing of 0.34 nm and 0.21 nm, assigning to (002) and (100) of the crystal plane of graphite (JCPDS 26–1076), respectively [49,50]. In addition, the diffraction peaks at 27.8°, 44.6°, and 53.1° could be well indexed as the (220), (101), (004) reflections of graphite (JCPDS 26–1076). Same phenomena were also reported by previous studies [51–53]. Fig. 2G shows the Raman spectrum of the prepared H-CDs, exhibiting two peaks at 1265 and 1587 cm−1, which are attributed to the disordered band (D band) and the graphitic band (G band). The intensity ratio of two peaks (ID/IG) was about 0.8, proving the high crystallinity structure of the H-CDs [54,55].

The morphologies and the size distribution of the H-CDs and the H-CDs aggregates were characterized using TEM and DLS. The TEM image of the H-CDs was displayed in Fig. 2H, and the corresponding particle diameters (inset of Fig. 2H) were between 0.8 and 7 nm. After adding 90% (v/v) water, the H-CDs aggregates (Fig. 2I) were observed as a compact sheet structure, with an average diameter of about 740.7 nm. Overall, the above results proved the successful preparation of H-CDs.

The photoluminescence (PL) excitation, emission, UV–vis, and PL lifetime of the prepared H-CDs were measured to investigate their optical properties. Fig. 2J showed the PL excitation and emission of the as-prepared H-CDs in EtOH solution, exhibiting a blue emission (440 nm) with excitation wavelengths of 376 nm. As shown in Fig. 2K, the absorption peaks of the H-CDs EtOH solution appeared at 252 and 312 nm, attributing to the π-π* transition of C = C in the core of the H-CDs. After adding 90% water, the characteristic bands appeared at 252 and 345 nm. The absorption band at 312 nm was red-shift by 33 nm after adding water, which indicated that this absorption band was related to the aggregation of H-CDs. Owing to the hydrophobicity of H-CDs, H-CDs aggregates were formed in the solution with high water fraction, resulting from the restriction of intramolecular rotation and activation of the AIE process [7,47]. In addition, the fluorescence QY of H-CDs was 1% in EtOH solution and 3.8% in 90% (v/v) water. Fig. 2L showed that the PL decay of H-CDs in EtOH solution was lower than that in 90% (v/v) water. The average PL lifetime increased from 2.8 ns to 5.4 ns after adding 90% (v/v) water, meaning that the formation of H-CDs aggregates contributed to the increase in PL lifetime.

In light of the hydrophobicity, the formation of H-CDs aggregates was observed in a solution with high water fractions (fw). As shown in Fig. 2M and N, the fluorescence property of H-CDs in different volumetric ratios of water/EtOH solutions was explored. When the fw in the H-CDs solution was less than 50%, the H-CDs solution exhibited a clear pink solution under natural light, and it emitted blue fluorescence under UV light. Meanwhile, the corresponding fluorescence spectra only displayed an emission peak at 440 nm (Fig. 2N). With the fw increased (more than 30%), the H-CDs aggregated was formed and the solution changed from transparent to turbid, and the suspension exhibited light-pink emission. A new emission peak was observed at 615 nm, due to red fluorescence. With the further increase in fw, the fluorescence of the solution gradually changed from light pink to pink, and then to red. In addition, with the increase in fw, the emission peak of H-CDs solution at 440 nm was red-shift, accompanied by the gradual decrease of the PL intensity, while the red emission at 615 nm continued to increase. The PL intensity ratio of two emission peaks showed a positive correlation with fw. This AIE phenomenon could be attributed to the restriction of intramolecular motion and the enhancement of the intramolecular charge transfer effect in the higher water fractions [44,56]. The above results confirmed that the as-prepared H-CDs had hydrophobicity and AIE properties, and their excellent optical property provided the possibility to fabricate an H-CDs-based ratiometric fluorescent sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of H-CDs aggregates for detection of VBNs

ce:section.ce:para: After exploring the sensing performance of the H-CDs aggregates toward VBNs, the sensing mechanism of the as-prepared sensor was explored by UV–vis, DLS, TEM, XRD, IR, and XPS. Fig. 3D shows the UV–vis spectra of H-CDs aggregates with or without NH3 and spermine. Compared with H-CDs aggregates, the absorption peaks at 345 nm, relating to the aggregation of H-CDs, were blue-shift after adding NH3 and spermine, indicating the disaggregation of the H-CDs aggregates. As revealed in Fig. 3E, the diameters of the H-CDs aggregates gradually decreased as the concentrations of NH3 increased, identifying with the results from UV–vis spectra, which confirmed disaggregation of the H-CDs aggregates after adding VBNs. With the addition of ammonia and spermine, the absolute values of zeta potential increased, indicating that the reaction system were electrically stabilized (Fig. S1). This result proved the disaggregation of the H-CDs aggregates after adding VBNs. The TEM images were recorded to further prove the disaggregation of H-CDs aggregates. As shown in Fig. 3F, in presence of NH3, the compact layered structure of H-CDs aggregates (Fig. 2I) was destroyed, and the smaller nanoribbons with the graphene-like structure were obtained. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (inset of Fig. 3F) revealed that the obtained H-CDs showed well-resolved lattice fringes with the lattice spacing of 0.34 nm, attributing to the (002) plane lattice of graphene [45,49]. After adding NH3, the XRD pattern (Fig. 3G) showed a broad peak at about 23°, representing the 0.34 nm interlayer spacing, which was in keeping with the results obtained from the TEM image [45]. In addition, Fig. 3H reveals the Raman spectrum of the H-CDs aggregates after adding NH3. The intensity ratio of the two main peaks (ID/IG) was about 2.4, which was higher than that of the reported graphene materials (1.05), revealing that it possessed a disordered graphite-like structure, which was similar to the common CDs [42]. These results confirmed that the original structure of H-CDs aggregates was destroyed after reacting with VBNs.

Then, the IR spectra of H-CDs aggregates in presence of NH3 were also recorded, as depicted in Fig. 3I. The band at 3194 cm−1, assigned to N-H stretching vibration, appeared after reacting with NH3, confirming the binding of VBNs on the surface of H-CDs aggregates. The appearance of the –NH group would change the hydrophobicity of H-CDs, caused the de-agglomeration of H-CDs aggregates in an aqueous solution and then passivated their AIE property. Meanwhile, the elemental species and contents of the H-CDs aggregates after adding NH3 were detected by XPS and elemental analysis (Fig. 3J). The XPS survey spectrum showed the existence of O (1s), N (1s), C (1s), and S (2s and 2p) in the H-CDs aggregates after adding NH3. The corresponding atomic ratios were 33.58%, 2.52%, 56.19% and 7.71%, respectively [47]. Compared with the H-CDs (Fig. 2B), the atomic ratios of the N element increased significantly after reacting with NH3. As shown in Fig. 3K, compared with H-CDs aggregates, the atomic ratios of the N element and H element increased after adding VBNs, indicating that the VBNs were successfully combined to the surface of H-CDs aggregates, which was in line with the results from IR and XPS analysis. Therefore, the sensing mechanism of H-CDs aggregates toward VBNs could be summarized as the following fact: after reacting with VBNs, the appearance of the –NH group on the H-CDs could change the hydrophobicity of original H-CDs, resulting in the dissolution of the H-CDs aggregates in aqueous solution, which passivated AIE property of H-CDs aggregates and then caused the decrease of the PL intensity at 615 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the loaded H-CDs aggregates tag and application for VBNs monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: After exploring the sensing performance of H-CDs aggregates solution toward VBNs as well as confirming its detection mechanism, the loaded H-CDs aggregates tag was prepared (Fig. 1) and applied to monitor VBNs vapour (Fig. 4 B). As shown in Fig. S2, under natural light, the colour of the fibre cotton paper before and after being modified by H-CDs aggregates are white and light-pink, respectively. These colour variations reflected the successful deposition of H-CDs on the fibre cotton paper. Meanwhile, no fluorescence emission was observed from the fibre cotton paper, while the loaded H-CDs aggregates tag exhibited red fluorescence, which further proved that the H-CDs aggregates were successfully modified on the fibre cotton paper. In addition, SEM images were employed to characterize the successful deposition of the H-CDs aggregates on the fibre cotton paper. Fig. S2 showed that the original fibre cotton paper presented a multilayer loosely porous structure composed of fibres. After the deposition of H-CDs aggregates, many large particles were observed on the fibre surface, indicating that H-CDs could form larger aggregates on the surface of fibre cotton paper after the solvent was volatilized. It once again confirmed the successful modification of H-CDs aggregates.

The selectivity of the loaded H-CDs aggregates tag was investigated using eight volatile compounds (EtOH, H2S, H2O, MeOH, TEA, EtOAc, IPA, and NH3) produced during seafood spoilage as model targets. In particular, TEA and NH3 are two typical VBNs. The fluorescence response of the tag to different volatile compounds could also be quantitated by the R/B value, as shown in Fig. 4. Fig. 4A (Ⅰ) shows that the fabricated tag emitted red under UV light (Blank). After being treated with different volatile compounds for 3 min, it can be seen that the fluorescence colour of tags of EtOH, H2S, H2O, MeOH, EtOAc, and IPA was unchanged, while the fluorescence colour of tags of TEA and NH3 were going to be blue, which could be easily observed from the changes of R/B value in Fig. 4A (Ⅱ). Thus, the fabricated loaded H-CDs tag has good selectivity for VBNs.

To study the sensitivity of the presented tag for VBNs vapour detection, ammonia vapour was applied as the model target to investigate the fluorescence colour changes of the tags in the following two respects: (Ⅰ) treated with ammonia vapour (4000 ppm) for 3 min at the interval of 30 s (Fig. 4B); (Ⅱ) treated with ammonia vapour with different concentrations (0–4000 ppm) for 2 min (Fig. 4C). The results indicated that the colour of the tags gradually varied from red to pink, and then to blue under UV light with the treatment time prolonged, as well as the tags gradually transformed to pink, and then to blue under UV light by increasing the ammonia vapour concentration (2 min). Especially, there was a good linear correlation (y = −0.0003c + 1.3483, R2 = 0.987) between the R/B value and the concentrations of ammonia vapour in the range of 0–2000 ppm, with a calculated LOD of 317.8 ppm (S/N = 3).

In addition, the stability of the prepared tag was also studied, as shown in Fig. S3, after storage at room temperature for 4 months, no fluorescence colour change was observed, indicating the good stability of the presented tags. With the help of its portability, sensitivity, and stability of the tag, the prepared tag possesses a good potential for visual recognition of VBNs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of preparation condition of the loaded H-CDs aggregates tag
ce:section.ce:para: The concentrations of the H-CDs were optimized to obtain the optimum detection performance. As shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescent of the fabricated tag varied from pink to red, and then to dark red with the increase in the H-CDs concentrations. After being stored at 28 °C for 10 h, the total VBNs content in shrimps was 30.47 mg/100 g (Fig. 5 A (b)), indicating the severely spoiled status of the shrimp. For group A (3 mg/mL), the fluorescence colour changes were difficult to be observed with the naked eye during the first 4 h. For group D (4.5 mg/mL), after12 h of storage, the endpoint colour did not change to blue. Thus, groups A and D were not able to employed for recognition of shrimp freshness. In contrast, group B (3.5 mg/mL) and group C (4 mg/mL) exhibited a relatively clear fluorescent colour change. In comparison with group B, the fluorescence colour variations of group C were easier to be observed with the naked eye. Thus, the optimal H-CDs concentration was 4 mg/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the loaded H-CDs aggregates tag for monitoring VBNs in shrimp
ce:section.ce:para: In view of the above results, the loaded H-CDs aggregates tags were prepared and applied to investigate their ability for in-situ monitoring shrimp freshness. As shown in Fig. 5A, by storing the shrimp at 4 (Fig. 5A (a)) and 28 °C (Fig. 5A (b)), the dynamic changes of shrimp were recorded by smartphone. The fluorescence response of the tag toward VBNs in shrimps could also be quantitated by R/B value, as shown in Fig. 5A (a-Ⅱ) and Fig. 5A (b-Ⅱ). In addition, a colour card (Fig. 5A (c)) was prepared based on the relationship between the colour changes of the presented tags as well as the total VBNs in shrimps, providing an intuitive way for visual recognition by the naked eye.

As revealed in Fig. 5A (a), the initial total VBNs value was 7.59 mg/100 g, reflecting the fresh status of shrimp, and the fluorescent colour of the tag was red, accompanied by an R/B value of more than 1. As the storage period prolonging, the fluorescent colour changed to light-red when the total VBNs content was in the range of 10–20 mg/100 g, and the corresponding R/B value was in the range of 0.9–1, indicating the second fresh states of shrimp. When the storage time was further extended to 3, 4, and 5 d, the values increased to 22.31, 25.95, and 30.74 mg/100 g and the fluorescent colour changed to pink, turning to blue, as well as then to blue, and the corresponding R/B value was in the range of 0.7–0.9, 0.4–0.7, and less than 0.4, indicating the different status of decomposed, slight spoiled, and severe spoiled, respectively. More importantly, the fluorescent colour variations of the presented loaded H-CDs aggregates tag showed the same trend after being treated with shrimp at 28 °C (Fig. 5A (b)).

The above results proved that the loaded H-CDs aggregates tag could be applied for in-site, rapid, visual recognition of shrimp freshness, as well as the fluorescent colour proving the freshness could be divided into five gradients (Fig. 5A (c)), with red, light red, pink, localized blue, and blue representing fresh, second fresh, decomposed, slight spoiled, as well as severe spoiled, respectively. Thus, with the help of the designed colour card, the fabricated loaded H-CDs aggregates tag could be easily applied for monitoring shrimp freshness in a visual and in-situ manner.

To further explore the accuracy of the fabricated loaded H-CDs aggregates tag, the tags were applied to monitor the freshness of shrimp. Fig. 5B exhibited the applications of the loaded H-CDs aggregates tag to assess the shrimp freshness at 28 (Fig. 5B (a)) and 4 °C (Fig. 5B (b)). For storage at 28 °C, the total VBNs values of group A was 13.63 mg/100 g, and the fluorescence of the tag was light-red, accompanied by the R/B value of 0.97, which was in line with the established colour card (Fig. 5A (c)). Meanwhile, the total VBNs values grew to 23.89 mg/100 g (group B) and 27.10 mg/100g (group C) as well as the fluorescence of the tags was pink and turning to blue, as well as the corresponding R/B values were 0.87 and 0.74, respectively, implying a variation in states to decomposed and slightly spoiled.

Meanwhile, the fabricated loaded H-CDs aggregates tags were also applied for recognition of shrimp freshness at 4 °C. As revealed in Fig. 5B (b), the total VBNs contents of group D, group E, and group F were 15.48 mg/100 g, 24.56 mg/100 g, and 30.88 mg/100 g, and the corresponding colours were light-red, pink, and blue under UV light, as well as the R/B values, were 0.96, 0.82, and 0.32, implying different status of second fresh, decomposed, as well as severe spoiled, respectively. Thus, the results proved that the fabricated tag can be used as an effective gas sensor for non-destructive recognition of packaging seafood freshness.

The potential application of the loaded H-CDs aggregates tag in the seafood freshness assessment depended on their cytotoxicity. In this work, the CCK8 assay was applied to investigate the cytotoxicity of the H-CDs and the viability of 293T cells. Fig. 6 exhibited the CCK8 assay results for negative control (NC), positive control (PC), 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, and 4 mg/mL H-CDs after being incubated with 293T cells for 24 h. The results showed that the cell viabilities for the H-CDs with different concentrations were higher than that of NC and PC, indicating that the as-prepared H-CDs had the ability to promote cell viability and growth, which also confirmed the nontoxicity of the as-prepared H-CDs. Therefore, the above results indicated that the H-CDs could be expected as a good ratiometric probe for the construction of fluorescent tags to monitor seafood freshness.

A dual-emissive ratiometric fluorescent tag was constructed based on the H-CDs aggregates with AIE properties in this work for monitoring seafood freshness in in-situ and visually. On the basis of its good luminescent property, the sensitivity of the H-CDs aggregates toward VBNs was explored using spermine and ammonium hydroxide as typical targets, where the calculated LODs was 7 μM and 137 ppb for spermine and ammonium hydroxide, respectively. The sensing mechanism was ascribed to passivate the AIE properties of H-CDs aggregates after reacting with VBNs. Based on it, a dual-emissive ratiometric tag was fabricated and used for visual recognition of ammonia vapour, exhibiting highly recognizable colour variations from red to blue under UV light. To explore its feasibility and applicability, the presented tags were used to monitor the seafood freshness in a real-time and visual manner, and the results of colour changes were consistent with the total VBNs value. In addition, cytotoxicity analysis based on CCK8 assay proved the nontoxicity of the prepared H-CDs. Thus, this work might offer a new avenue to establish a ratiometric fluorescent sensor.
